summary: Well-engineered weight correction implementation. The compile-time sRGB-to-linear LUT is clever — avoids runtime pow() in no_std via Newton's method for fifth roots in const context. WCAG-based luminance calculation is standards-compliant. Continuous linear correction curve (not binary) matches the spec requirement. 15% max reduction is a reasonable perceptual ceiling. Clean no-op path for non-variable fonts. 10 tests thoroughly cover all validation assertions including the critical monotonicity property.

observations: Compile-time LUT computation is sophisticated but well-commented with the math derivation
Newton iteration for fifth root has proper convergence guards (50 iterations, epsilon check)
The WCAG contrast ratio formula is correctly implemented (lighter + 0.05) / (darker + 0.05)
Function signatures are ergonomic — separate r/g/b components avoid unnecessary struct allocation
The 0.15 max reduction constant is documented with its rationale
Doc comments are thorough, explaining the irradiation phenomenon and the correction model
sRGB-to-linear conversion uses correct formula with 0.04045 threshold
Relative luminance uses correct BT.709 coefficients (0.2126, 0.7152, 0.0722)
Factor is correctly bounded to [0.85, 1.0] range
auto_weight_correction_axes correctly applies factor to default_value and clamps to axis range
Early return when factor == 1.0 avoids unnecessary axis variation overhead
fg_lum <= bg_lum check correctly returns 1.0 for dark-on-light (no correction needed)
VAL-WEIGHT-002: 5 contrast levels with strictly monotonic decrease — verified
VAL-WEIGHT-003: Actual rasterized coverage reduction at wght=400 base — verified
VAL-WEIGHT-004: No-op for both static fonts and empty data, byte-identical rendering — verified
No user input, no IPC changes, pure computation

nonBlockingIssues.description: The SRGB_TO_LINEAR_LUT uses a clever Newton iteration at compile time, but the comment says 'Taylor series' which is inaccurate — it's actually Newton's method for root finding. Minor documentation inconsistency.
